Subject: Re: Churn & Risk Ideas

 

So as examples of revenue migration:

  1. Customer A moves from Managed Hosting to Azure, managed hosting consumption is replaced by Azure consumption and/or managed cloud
  1. Customer B moves from old Managed hosting to refreshed hardware, but re-architectured with less capacity, so lower MRC

 

These revenue erosion are still important to highlite to finance, but most likely (?) do not require a risk management (maybe with some exceptions where risk is introduced).  

 

 

Getting into solutioning here, trying to progress on getting this started...

 

If we're to track this in Salesforce, I think we'd need to ensure we can easily capture the following information:

  • Identify the opportunity as a migration (Type= Migration)
  • MRC of the new service
  • Service it replaces (with ServiceIDs?)
    • From there we can infer what is the current MRC it replaces

 

 

Thoughts?  

 

 

I think there may also be implication on the compensation plan, but to be tackled later...

From: Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>
Sent: Friday, February 20, 2026 10:25 AM
To: Sarah Blanchard <Sarah.Blanchard@aptum.com>; Ian Rae <Ian.Rae@aptum.com>
Cc: Frederic Gingras <Frederic.Gingras@aptum.com>
Subject: Churn & Risk Ideas

 

Sarah/Ian,

 

Just had a good chat with Fred about churn and risk. I think part of the reason the risk list feels noisy/useless right now is that we’re mixing two different things around what “churn” actually is.

 

Here’s what we discussed, redefining churn into two separate things:

 

Revenue Migration

  • Customer still needs the underlying functionality although delivered differently (different product/service).
  • Example: On prem → Cloud. Same business need, different backbone.
  • Revenue may shift buckets or even drop, but the customer relationship & value is retained.

 

Revenue Attrition / Loss

  • Customer removes or reduces a service because they don’t see enough value in Aptum delivering it.
    • This applies even if they keep other services with us.
  • Example: Dropping advanced monitoring to basic, or cancelling a service entirely.

 

Tactically:

  • Revenue Attrition / Loss = true risk. These belong in Jira and the Risk Registe where the SDMs and the technical team can (hopefully) proactively influence outcome.
  • Revenue Migration = shift. These should be identified upstream in Salesforce and visible in Deal Desk. If we’re losing on-prem, we should clearly see the cloud replacement opportunity. If we don’t, then it’s actually loss.

 

If we separate these cleanly, I think:

  • I can focus the Risk Register on real service risk and look to improve products and service delivery.
  • Fred can manage migration dynamics with his team.
  • Finance gets a clearer view of actual attrition vs revenue mix shift.

 

Curious if this framing makes sense. If we can align on this I can ratchet up my expectations of that risk list. 

 

Adam Nelson

Senior Director, Service Delivery

Subject: Action required: Meet your eligibility requirements for CSP direct bill
 
90-day notification: Your CSP direct bill authorization may be at-risk.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     

Your organization is not meeting the CSP direct bill requirements

March 26, 2026

This notification is regarding your company’s Microsoft Cloud Solution Provider (CSP) direct bill authorization with tenant ID AE111DB6-0F6F-45FA-A108-ECA3FEBF49ED. You may download the applicable agreement on the agreements page of your CSP Partner Center tenant. 

On May 01, 2025, we announced minimum eligibility requirements for CSP direct bill partners. Starting on October 01, 2025, direct bill partners must meet the following requirements by the anniversary month* of your onboarding as a CSP direct bill partner (90 days from this notice). Details on how to identify your onboarding month can be found here.  

Minimum eligibility requirements:  

  • Have at least USD one million in CSP trailing 12 months (TTM) transactional revenue at the Partner Global Account (PGA) level. Partners can review their TTM revenue in the CSP Revenue Details report in the “downloads hub” of Partner Center Insights workspace. The sales that make up your USD one million TTM revenue are subject to review and verification by Microsoft, and you might be asked to provide additional information. The revenue requirement is calculated based on cloud revenue and excludes perpetual software in CSP. 
  • At least one MAICPP Solution Partner designation.   

Complete mandatory security requirements listed below: 

  • Enable multifactor authentication (MFA) for all administrative users in the CSP tenant. 
  • Designate a security contact within Partner Center. 
  • Respond to security alerts within 24 hours or less.
  • Purchase of a Microsoft Advanced Support for Partners plan or a Microsoft Premier Support for Partners plan.

Our records show that your organization currently is not meeting one or more of the above direct bill requirements. 

Required action 

To continue operating as an authorized direct bill partner, your organization is required to meet the above eligibility requirements by the anniversary month of your onboarding as a direct bill partner for this tenant (90 days from this notice). If your direct bill tenant does not meet the eligibility requirement by your anniversary month, your tenant’s direct bill authorization may be at risk of being terminated. If that occurs, this tenant will be restricted, and you will have 30 days to assist customers in transitioning to an alternative CSP Partner.

For more information on Deauthorization and Change of Channel Partner (COCP) actions, please see: Link to CSP Authorization FAQ 

*Anniversary month is based on the earliest CSP enrollment date of all tenants under the Partner Global Account (PGA), not the MPA acceptance date shown in Partner Center for a particular CSP tenant/enrollment. We are working to ensure that this information is easily accessible in Partner Center.

What does this mean for you:

  • Notifications reflect the earliest CSP enrollment date across all tenants, across all CSP regions under your PGA.
  • To confirm your anniversary month, review all CSP enrollments at the PGA level.

We look forward to continued partnership with you in CSP. 

Privacy Statement

data.subject: ATTENTION: Experience the New Personalized Salesforce My Trust Center

body.content:
Product & Service Notification

Say goodbye to alert fatigue and generic status updates. We're thrilled to introduce Salesforce My Trust Center, your new authenticated portal for everything you need to know about your service health.

Unlike the public Trust and Status sites, My Trust Center is tailored specifically to your implementation, centralizing important communications and eliminating "noise" by only showing updates relevant to your specific orgs and tenants.
What makes My Trust Center different
  • Get Instant Tenant Status: Check the real-time health of your tenants as soon as you log in.
  • Keep Track of Maintenance and Releases: View and plan for your upcoming scheduled maintenance windows and releases.
  • Access Historical Insights: Securely view history—up to 1 full year—such as patch release notes, maintenance events, and incidents.
Get started with My Trust Center today
My Trust Center is live for Agentforce 360 Platform, Data 360, Salesforce AI, Agentforce Sales, Agentforce Service, Marketing Cloud Engagement, and Salesforce Industries, with other Salesforce products onboarding throughout the year. Getting started is easy.

  1. Navigate to my.trust.salesforce.com and register using your Salesforce Trailblazer account.
  2. Check out Upcoming Maintenance to view planned maintenance over the next 90 days.
  3. Navigate to Tenants to view all orgs assigned to you. Click View Details to see past, current, and future events and incidents on the individual tenant.
  4. Click Subscriptions. Here, you can subscribe to informational messages as well as customize your tenant-level notifications. Simply click Subscribe to Tenant, select the tenant, and choose the event types (like incidents, patch releases, or maintenance windows) you want to receive notifications.
Note: Existing Salesforce Trust subscriptions do not transfer over to My Trust Center. You will continue to receive Trust notifications until Status is fully decommissioned.
Learn more about My Trust Center
For additional information, check out the My Trust Center FAQs, and be on the lookout for an upcoming Salesforce Admin blog post.

We will also host webinars walking through the new experience and answering questions. Register to join.
  • Wednesday April 1 - 15:00 UTC - https://salesforce.zoom.us/j/82737823896?pwd=zmzTcraVkuU5VSaPxUfaPsEkG6Ylvo.1

data.subject: Centrilogic & Aptum - Meeting Minutes – Bracknell update & Strategy - 26-03-30

body.content:
Overview of Customer Footprint
  • The majority of customers are hosted in standard Centrilogic cabinets using shared power and carrier infrastructure.
  • One customer operates IBM mainframe equipment housed in non‑standard cabinets (wider and deeper), requiring confirmation of:
    • Power requirements
    • Weight and handling constraints
  • Only one customer (Perforce) operates within a dedicated cage (three cabinets plus expansion capacity).
  • Several customers also occupy office space in addition to data centre cabinets, which may influence their migration decisions.

Customer‑Specific Observations
  • Digital AI
    • Uses Centrilogic cabinets.
    • Requires physical separation between cabinets due to Wi‑Fi interference constraints.
  • Cloudian
    • Operates three cabinets with relatively high power consumption.
    • Also uses office space, though this is primarily for convenience.
    • It was agreed that, if migrating, the focus would likely be on rack space only, not office space.
  • Infinity
    • Uses one cabinet and office space.
    • Staff are onsite daily, making a migration to Portsmouth potentially less attractive.
  • OpenText
    • Uses six cabinets (five active, one for storage).
    • Makes frequent use of Smart Hands, which is included in their contract.
  • Perforce
    • Only cage customer at Bracknell.
    • Likely to seek an alternative location closer to their existing office, though requirements will be clarified in case migration is considered.
  • Telco providers (e.g., CityFibre, Colt)
    • Not expected to migrate to Aptum.
    • Will require time to remove or migrate their downstream customers from Bracknell.

Likelihood of Migration to Aptum
  • The general assessment is that more than 50% of customers may choose to migrate to Aptum.
  • A key differentiator will be the availability of migration assistance services, including:
    • De‑racking
    • Transportation
    • Re‑racking
    • Optional re‑cabling and Smart Hands support
  • Providing structured migration support is expected to significantly increase customer uptake.

Information Required to Progress
Aptum requested that the customer tracking spreadsheet include the following information for each customer: Number of cabinets and approximate REUs
  • Power provisioned and, where available, estimated power utilisation
  • Power feeds (A/B), number of circuits
  • Connectivity details:
    • Port speeds (1G, 10G)
    • Bandwidth utilisation
    • Use of Centrilogic carriers vs. customer‑owned circuits
  • IP address information:
    • IP blocks assigned (e.g. /29)
    • Confirmation of inclusion within the proposed /24 block
  • Contact status:
    • Whether the customer has already been contacted
    • By whom and when

Connectivity and IP Considerations
  • Most customers rely on Centrilogic carrier mix (Telia, LINX, Cogent, Virgin, Vodafone).
  • A small number of customers use customer‑owned last‑mile circuits (primarily BT or Virgin).
  • No major technical blockers were identified:
    • Aptum has sufficient space and power capacity.
    • Carrier compatibility appears feasible.
  • Follow‑up is required to:
    • Confirm power requirements for the IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility for migrating customers.
    • Confirm treatment of the /24 IP block for customer continuity.

Customer Communications
  • Two draft customer notification letters have been prepared but have not yet been sent.
  • Formal communication is intentionally being held until landlord agreements are finalised.
  • It was agreed that:
    • Aptum should not contact customers directly until a formal introduction is made by Centrilogic.
    • The draft letter will be shared with Aptum for review (already shared).
    • Aptum will contribute language describing:
      • Facility capabilities
      • Migration assistance services
  • The previously referenced June 30 date is not yet firm and remains subject to landlord agreement.

Key Agreements
  • No critical technical blockers were identified for the majority of customers.
  • Migration assistance is essential to the success of customer transitions.
  • The enriched customer spreadsheet is the critical dependency for next steps.
  • Early and coordinated communication will be key once formal notices are issued.


Action Items
  • Sergio B
    • Update and enrich the customer spreadsheet. - ONGOING
    • Share the spreadsheet and draft customer letter in the agreed shared folder. DONE
  • Aptum
    • Review the draft customer communication.
    • Provide proposed language covering facility details and migration services. @Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com,  @george.revie@aptum.com  would it be possible to receive the revised version by end of day today (3/30)?
  • Network & Facilities
    • Confirm power requirements for IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility.
  • All parties
    • Re‑engage once landlord agreements allow formal customer communication.

Please feel free to add any comments or suggestions.

SERGIO BERNARDOU

Project Management Consultant


sbernardou@centrilogic.com

www.centrilogic.com

Follow us: Linkedin | X

Hi Aptum Team,

 

And happy Friday! Thank you again for your time last week to go over the solution concepts we discussed and how you can utilize Megaport to your end customers for all things connectivity. Following up, please see action items below:

  1. Solution Overview - Attached is a consolidated layout of the different connectivity scenarios we can look at your positioning to your customers with any kind of connectivity requirements. Including:
    1. Data Center to Data Center
    2. Connecting to Aptum Private Cloud (from any A-End point i.e. their HQ, another DC, public cloud, etc.). Documentation on utiilizing our Marketplace for this can be found here. 
    3. Public Cloud Connectivity
  1. APIs - As mentioned, you can utilize our public APIs to integrate these product suites directly into your own customer console. 
    1. Documentation
    2. Public APIs
    3. Example of a partner doing it today
  1. Resale Partnership - On top of this, you can become a reseller and get access to our Partner Portal that gives you the ability to create dedicated accounts for all of your customers (as well as resale margin on all orders)

Please review and let us know if you'd like to get some time to sync up on next steps. Looking forward to it!

 

Andrew Coggins
Director - Data Center Development

To: Marc-Alexandre.Forget@aptum.com <Marc-Alexandre.Forget@aptum.com>; Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com <Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com>; Adam.Nelson@aptum.com <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>; george.revie@aptum.com <george.revie@aptum.com>; Peter Bell <pbell@centrilogic.com>; Joshua Justice <jjustice@centrilogic.com>; Kevin Aplin <KAplin@centrilogic.com>; Karin Vierhuis <kvierhuis@centrilogic.com>; Mark Moore <MMoore@centrilogic.com>
Cc: Martin Mossakowski <mmossakowski@centrilogic.com>
Subject: Centrilogic & Aptum - Meeting Minutes – Bracknell update & Strategy - 26-03-30
 
Overview of Customer Footprint
  • The majority of customers are hosted in standard Centrilogic cabinets using shared power and carrier infrastructure.
  • One customer operates IBM mainframe equipment housed in non‑standard cabinets (wider and deeper), requiring confirmation of:
    • Power requirements
    • Weight and handling constraints
  • Only one customer (Perforce) operates within a dedicated cage (three cabinets plus expansion capacity).
  • Several customers also occupy office space in addition to data centre cabinets, which may influence their migration decisions.

Customer‑Specific Observations
  • Digital AI
    • Uses Centrilogic cabinets.
    • Requires physical separation between cabinets due to Wi‑Fi interference constraints.
  • Cloudian
    • Operates three cabinets with relatively high power consumption.
    • Also uses office space, though this is primarily for convenience.
    • It was agreed that, if migrating, the focus would likely be on rack space only, not office space.
  • Infinity
    • Uses one cabinet and office space.
    • Staff are onsite daily, making a migration to Portsmouth potentially less attractive.
  • OpenText
    • Uses six cabinets (five active, one for storage).
    • Makes frequent use of Smart Hands, which is included in their contract.
  • Perforce
    • Only cage customer at Bracknell.
    • Likely to seek an alternative location closer to their existing office, though requirements will be clarified in case migration is considered.
  • Telco providers (e.g., CityFibre, Colt)
    • Not expected to migrate to Aptum.
    • Will require time to remove or migrate their downstream customers from Bracknell.

Likelihood of Migration to Aptum
  • The general assessment is that more than 50% of customers may choose to migrate to Aptum.
  • A key differentiator will be the availability of migration assistance services, including:
    • De‑racking
    • Transportation
    • Re‑racking
    • Optional re‑cabling and Smart Hands support
  • Providing structured migration support is expected to significantly increase customer uptake.

Information Required to Progress
Aptum requested that the customer tracking spreadsheet include the following information for each customer: Number of cabinets and approximate REUs
  • Power provisioned and, where available, estimated power utilisation
  • Power feeds (A/B), number of circuits
  • Connectivity details:
    • Port speeds (1G, 10G)
    • Bandwidth utilisation
    • Use of Centrilogic carriers vs. customer‑owned circuits
  • IP address information:
    • IP blocks assigned (e.g. /29)
    • Confirmation of inclusion within the proposed /24 block
  • Contact status:
    • Whether the customer has already been contacted
    • By whom and when

Connectivity and IP Considerations
  • Most customers rely on Centrilogic carrier mix (Telia, LINX, Cogent, Virgin, Vodafone).
  • A small number of customers use customer‑owned last‑mile circuits (primarily BT or Virgin).
  • No major technical blockers were identified:
    • Aptum has sufficient space and power capacity.
    • Carrier compatibility appears feasible.
  • Follow‑up is required to:
    • Confirm power requirements for the IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility for migrating customers.
    • Confirm treatment of the /24 IP block for customer continuity.

Customer Communications
  • Two draft customer notification letters have been prepared but have not yet been sent.
  • Formal communication is intentionally being held until landlord agreements are finalised.
  • It was agreed that:
    • Aptum should not contact customers directly until a formal introduction is made by Centrilogic.
    • The draft letter will be shared with Aptum for review (already shared).
    • Aptum will contribute language describing:
      • Facility capabilities
      • Migration assistance services
  • The previously referenced June 30 date is not yet firm and remains subject to landlord agreement.

Key Agreements
  • No critical technical blockers were identified for the majority of customers.
  • Migration assistance is essential to the success of customer transitions.
  • The enriched customer spreadsheet is the critical dependency for next steps.
  • Early and coordinated communication will be key once formal notices are issued.


Action Items
  • Sergio B
    • Update and enrich the customer spreadsheet. - ONGOING
    • Share the spreadsheet and draft customer letter in the agreed shared folder. DONE
  • Aptum
    • Review the draft customer communication.
    • Provide proposed language covering facility details and migration services. @Bradley.Petrie@aptum.com,  @george.revie@aptum.com  would it be possible to receive the revised version by end of day today (3/30)?
  • Network & Facilities
    • Confirm power requirements for IBM mainframe equipment.
    • Validate carrier mix and cross‑connect compatibility.
  • All parties
    • Re‑engage once landlord agreements allow formal customer communication.

Please feel free to add any comments or suggestions.

SERGIO BERNARDOU

Project Management Consultant


sbernardou@centrilogic.com

www.centrilogic.com

Follow us: Linkedin | X

However, we’ve since received confirmation from Samar at Insight that Microsoft does not require SA for this specific customer scenario, as the hardware is physical and dedicated to the customer.
Given this clarification directly from Microsoft’s licensing channel, I’d like guidance on whether we should maintain our original stance for consistency and risk management or adjust our approach to reflect Microsoft’s position. Cc: Fred Taylor-Young <Fred.Taylor-Young@aptum.com>
Subject: RE: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification
 

The licensing problem involves the product key used to install SQL Server.  Microsoft gives the exact same product key to all Software Assurance (Enterprise)/MSDN (Developer)/SPLA (Hosting) customers.  Only retail product keys are unique.  Thus, in most cases, the product key is not proof of licensing.  In past audits, if Microsoft found this product key used for SQL Server, it assumed we provided the product key and thus licensing.  If we weren’t sending Microsoft their royalty for the license, we owed back licensing fees.

 

SQL Server + CAL is a retail-only product.  It is, to my knowledge, not offered under Software Assurance, nor can it offered under SPLA.

 

If a customer buys their own SQL Server licenses through Software Assurance, they have the ability to use these licenses in Azure, other public cloud, or a hosting provider.  This is done through License Mobility and requires the license holder to submit a form attesting that they have the required license.

 

Microsoft licensing is complex and my understanding is not complete.  However, since it carries a real financial liability if we get it wrong, I believe it’s important that we understand what Microsoft allows and requires.  I’ve documents that I’ve used to make sense of this.

Firstly, I don't think we are in trouble here - I think we are only responsible if we supplied them with SPLA SQL licensing and we've used the wrong number of cores or incorrect edition etc. There are no SPLA licenses on their server in Ocean, so I don't think Microsoft would even check it on an audit. If they found SQL on it we could say that the customer is using BYOL and is responsible for their own licensing (we have tickets as proof we've asked), and then they would have to produce their licenses to MS and be fined if they aren't covered correctly. @Carlton, have I got that correct?

 

Secondly, my understanding is that Charles may be incorrect (apologies Charles!), not sure if there was a recent change with the licensing models. My understanding is if this was a virtual server that can be moved between hosts, or is on a shared stack, or on our/their private cloud, or a newer version of SQL than what they are licensed for, the customer would 100% need SA on their licenses and would have to use the per core model based on the cores in the physical hosts. With Azure Arc or CSP licensing, you can now get away with just licensing the cores in the VM.

 

However, this is physical hardware dedicated to them with a Windows OS directly on top with presumably a fixed number of SQL connections, so they are allowed to use the Server license + CAL model, unless I'm missing something. @Charles was that information directly from an MS ticket? If not, we should probably open one to confirm if that model is allowed on a dedicated physical server in our data centers - I think it is now allowed.

 

In answer to the other questions, If their current licensing is confirmed as inadequate, when I compared the numbers SQL Arc would probably offer the cheapest SQL licensing for them as they can pay by the hour on their Azure consumption. If they want fixed costs, we can sell them CSP 1 year or 3 year options but the 1 year would be more expensive than Arc and the 3 year is about the same, so long as MS don't jack up their SQL PAYG pricing.

From: Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>
Sent: Friday, March 27, 2026 8:18:39 PM
To: David Pistacchio (Consultant) <David.Pistacchio@digitalbridge.com>
Cc: Sarah Blanchard <sblanchard@cloudops.com>
Subject: Billable Hours Calulcations
 
Dave,

Sarah mentioned you wanted some insight into the billable hours math I use. Happy to walk through the methodology, and I've included a couple of snapshots that show how it plays out in practice.

I don't use a flat 2,080 spread over the year. I build capacity bottom-up each month:

  1. Start with net working days in the month.
  2. Subtract planned vacations (a quick CSV export from ADP).
  3. Subtract company holidays. This is a bit more involved because of location nuances (UK, US, and each Canadian province has slightly different days off), but I only need to set it up once.
  4. That gives me workable time per person. I apply a utilization target by role: 80% for Professional Services engineers, 50% for Solution Architects.
  5. I total those up and add 15% capacity on top for SDM time, and that's our billable target for the month.

The reason I don't use 100% is that when engineers are rotating across 3-4 projects in a week, context switching makes it impossible to bill every hour. If they were embedded on a single engagement it would be different, but we don't have projects of that scale right now. 80% is a realistic, and a number that after 6 years here we seem to always end up at (and find is the sweet spot to plan around). 

Also, the reason I don't spread vacation evenly across 12 months is because a) it's usually noise, b) I have the data at my fingertips, might as well use it, and c) I need to plan delivery dates around weeks where someone is actually out, or where a company holiday takes the whole organization offline for a few days. This method gives me a much more accurate short-to-medium term forecast.


A few things to flag:
  • We are currently running at 86% utilization for March against an 80% target; currently over capacity.
  • Marcus and Nathan are fully committed to the Cofomo engagement (~300 hours/month between them). That engagement is a separate conversation, and I believe Sarah has given you some background. The short version is that at our $275 blended rate, those hours should be generating roughly $82K/month in PS revenue, but revenue was mostly captured on the front end, so unfortunately we are left capturing less revenue on the back end (hence the ~$38K). Overall on the project we are still above our blended rate, we just went way too hard early. Lesson learned. 
    • With those two committed to Cofomo, I effectively have Marc Hosang (plus Pat in a player/coach capacity) delivering everything else. That is roughly 4 equivalent delivery resources across ~20 active projects.
  • Tristan Kosciuch left the organization recently. We are backfilling with a more senior resource (architect level). Right now we need the team to be top-heavy. We don't have the luxury of carrying specialists who need constant architectural oversight from Pat. Marc Hosang is developing in that direction, but until the backfill happens, the bench is thin.
  • Where you see $0 in Rev. Delivered against planned revenue, that is typically a project where the original estimate (or a discounted rate) means we cannot recognize revenue above what the SOW allows. We still have to finish the work. 
  • This this the picture for March, as you can see we have ~20 projects in the hopper and with 2 of the 4 going almost full-time on one project that leaves Pat and Marc H to deliver the other 18. 


Happy to walk through any of this in more detail next week. Sarah mentioned you wanted to see the data before the weekend, so I wanted to get it over to you now.


Adam Nelson
